title: The Madness of Lord Tennyson
content:
The appointment of a new national poet is a dicey moment in the life of any country. Get it wrong, and the society's deepest spiritual diseases will go entirely untreated and undetected. The Victorians, despite their reputation for pomposity and windbag religiosity, surprisingly made the perfect choice in 1850. They chose Alfred, Lord Tennyson, a vagrant and depressed semi-atheist from a family of lunatics. Queen Victoria made him her poet laureate largely on the strength of his blockbuster elegy, In Memoriam.

Richard Holmes’s biography, The Boundless Deep, is chiefly concerned with this haunted and chaotic pre-fame poet. It tracks how young Tennyson metabolically absorbed the most disturbing ideas that contemporary science had to offer. Holmes, a master scholar-biographer, captures the whole man in lucid prose that effortlessly slips into profound interiority. His version of Tennyson is cosmically miserable but also highly appealing to modern sensibilities. The poet devoured works of popular science, finding the information as intoxicating as the romantic poetry of his idols.

Riding the fault line between historical epochs, Tennyson nervously absorbed how new discoveries were shifting human consciousness. Astronomy was deepening space, geology was deepening time, and he felt the answering tremor in his own soul. These scientific revelations brought a fearful new understanding of an empty heaven and a thoroughly disenchanted world. The new science of psychology also absorbed him deeply, making him a looming giant of modernity rather than a degraded Romantic. Standing between the Romantics and the Modernists, Tennyson perfectly captured the profound spiritual anxieties of his rapidly changing age.
